coredata.dc:title: Engineering excitation-independent turn-on fluorescent probe for mercury: Functionalized dendritic silica doped with red-emissive carbon dots towards simultaneous detection and remediation with biosensing application
coredata.dc:creator: Yadav, Sanjay
Choudhary, Nishu
Sonpal, Vasavdutta
Ranjan Paital, Alok
coredata.dc:description: The simultaneous detection and remediation of highly toxic mercury-ion are urgently needed for environmental benefits. However, most of the fluorescence probes for mercury-ion exhibit blue luminescence with fluorescence quenching, and the simultaneous functions are scarce. Therefore, a new strategy is adopted to observe turn-on fluorescence with mercury-ion using red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescence tag and alkyl thiol functionalized dendritic fibrous nano-silica (DFNS@SH) as a substrate for specificity. The newly constructed composite material (CD@DFNS@SH) shows excitation-independent selective turn-on red luminescence in the presence of mercury-ion with a LOD (limit of detection) value of 5 nM, which is well below the threshold limit prescribed by the World Health Organization (WHO) in drinking water. In addition, this material also shows a high adsorption capacity toward mercury remediation (695 mg g−1) from the aqueous system. The adsorption parameters are best fitted with the Langmuir model with a pseudo-second-order interaction mechanism. Furthermore, fluorescence imaging used the composite material for biosensing of mercury-ion in living organisms, Artemia Salina (brine shrimp). The material's real sample quantification of mercury-ion and recyclability are other advantages offered by this material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red carbon dots
Fibrous silica
Turn-on fluorescence probe
Mercury
Adsorption
Biosensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of sub nanometer dendritic fibrous nano silica (DFNS)
Loading of carbon dots and synthesis of the final material CD@DFNS@SH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India
Central Salt and Marine Chemicals Research Institute, Council of Scientific and Industrial Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: S.Y, N.C, V.S, A. R. P acknowledge the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Govt. of India, for the financial support (MLP 0055). S.Y. and N.C acknowledge CSIR & AcSIR for fellowship & Ph.D. degree. ADCIF division of CSIR-CSMCRI is acknowledged for providing analytical facilities. A CSIR-CSMCRI communication no 57/2023.

S.Y, N.C, V.S, A. R. P acknowledge the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Govt. of India, for the financial support (MLP 0055). S.Y. and N.C acknowledge CSIR & AcSIR for fellowship & Ph.D. degree. ADCIF division of CSIR-CSMCRI is acknowledged for providing analytical facilities. A CSIR-CSMCRI communication no 57/2023.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) TEM & HR-TEM images of the synthesized CDs (scale bar: 20 nm, 2 nm & 0.5 nm).

(A–C) The Fe-SEM images of the materials DFNS, DFNS@SH & CD@DFNS@SH (scale bar: 50 nm, 100 nm & 100 nm); (D-F) The TEM & HR-TEM images of the synthesized DFNS, DFNS@SH & CD@DFNS@SH materials, (scale bar: 0.2 µm (inset: 20 nm), 0.1 µm (inset: 20 nm), & 20 nm).
(A, B) Surface area & pore size measurement plots of the DFNS, DFNS@SH & CD@DFNS@SH materials. (C, D) Broad angle PXRD and thermogravimetric profiles of the DFNS, DFNS@SH & CD@DFNS@SH materials.
A comparison of the FTIR spectrum of the fibrous silica (DFNS), DFNS@SH & CD@DFNS@SH materials.
(A–D) The high-resolution C 1 s, N 1 s, O1s & Si 2p X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the final material CD@DFNS@SH.

The UV–Vis (A) & fluorescence spectra (B) of CD@DFNS@SH in an aqueous buffer medium in the presence of various metal ions. The selective fluorescence enhancement (C), Fluorescence titration (D); The non-linear fitting of titration data (inset picture: colour intensification of the probe material after addition of Hg2+ ions under far UV) (E); The LOD plot of the material towards Hg2+ (F); The interference studies with Hg2+ and other metal ions in excess within linear range (G); The stability of the material in the aqueous system (H) and the fluorescence lifetime profile (TCSPC) of the material CD@DFNS@SH before and after the addition of Hg2+ ions (I).
The schematic representation of the turn-on sensing mechanism of the material towards the Hg2+ ions.
(A) Equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) of the CD@DFNS@SH material towards Hg2+. (B) Langmuir adsorption isotherm of the material CD@DFNS@SH for Hg2+.

Adsorption Kinetics plots for Hg2+; (A) Concentration change as a function of time contact time. (B) Sorption capacity (qt) plot with time, inset (Pseudo seconder order kinetic plot with time with correlation factor (R2) = 0.99). (C) % Removal with time.
(A) The UV–Vis tracking of mercury-ion binding, acid-base induced striping, and regeneration of material for recyclability studies. (B) The adsorption capacities of the material for five cycles.

(A) Artemia nauplii under bright field image without probe materials. (B) Artemia nauplii under bright field image after ingestion of the material showing accumulation of the probe material in G.I tract. (C, D, E) Red staining of the Artemia nauplii treated with the probe material under a UV filter. (F, G, H) Artemia nauplii in the presence of probe and Hg2+ ion showed enhanced G.I tract's red staining. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The synthetic scheme for the preparation of red-emissive carbon dots (CDs), fibrous silica (DFNS), alkyl-thiol functionalized silica (DFNS@SH), and the carbon dot doped final material (CD@DFNS@SH).
BET parameters of the synthesized materials.
Comparison of the detection parameters of the different materials with Hg2+.

row.entry: An anthracene excimer fluorescence probe (mesoporous silica)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The thermodynamic parameters for the adsorption of Hg2+ ions (ΔH, ΔS & ΔG).

head.ce:title: Engineering excitation-independent turn-on fluorescent probe for mercury: Functionalized dendritic silica doped with red-emissive carbon dots towards simultaneous detection and remediation with biosensing application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Central Salt & Marine Chemicals Research Institute, G.B. Marg

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work demonstrates the dual functions of selective mercury-ion detection and remediation from an aqueous system using a single composite material. A new approach enabled turn-on fluorescence detection of mercury ions with very high sensitivity and showed a very high adsorption capacity of mercury-ion. The excitation-independency, red emission, recyclability, real sample quantification, and biosensing of mercury-ion are other advantages.

The simultaneous detection and remediation of highly toxic mercury-ion are urgently needed for environmental benefits. However, most of the fluorescence probes for mercury-ion exhibit blue luminescence with fluorescence quenching, and the simultaneous functions are scarce. Therefore, a new strategy is adopted to observe turn-on fluorescence with mercury-ion using red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescence tag and alkyl thiol functionalized dendritic fibrous nano-silica (DFNS@SH) as a substrate for specificity. The newly constructed composite material (CD@DFNS@SH) shows excitation-independent selective turn-on red luminescence in the presence of mercury-ion with a LOD (limit of detection) value of 5 nM, which is well below the threshold limit prescribed by the World Health Organization (WHO) in drinking water. In addition, this material also shows a high adsorption capacity toward mercury remediation (695 mg g−1) from the aqueous system. The adsorption parameters are best fitted with the Langmuir model with a pseudo-second-order interaction mechanism. Furthermore, fluorescence imaging used the composite material for biosensing of mercury-ion in living organisms, Artemia Salina (brine shrimp). The material's real sample quantification of mercury-ion and recyclability are other advantages offered by this material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion pollution is of great concern due to its lethality to human and ecological balance [1]. The Minamata incident in Japan due to mercury exposure resulted in several neurological disorders, multiple organ failures, and deaths [2]. Therefore, the mercury threshold in drinking water has been limited to 2 μg L−1 by World Health Organization (WHO). Thus, the detection & remediation of mercury is vital for environmental benefits. Considering the importance of the problem, efforts have been made to develop selective mercury detection, removal, and dual-functional platforms [3–7]. Among different detection methods, the fluorescence-based detection method has attracted attention due to its high sensitivity, easy operations, and cost-effectiveness. Therefore, efforts have been made to develop sensitive detection platforms such as organic fluorophores, nanomaterial probes, functional materials, and polymeric materials. Similarly, the adsorption-based remediation method is preferred for its operational easiness and effectiveness. Furthermore, efforts are given to designing inorganic–organic hybrid materials to bring synergy in the simultaneous sensing and removal of toxic analytes [8–10]. The hybrid materials require a porous solid matrix functionalized with an appropriate chelating organic fluorophore to target the analytes. Such materials have demonstrated several advantages over simple organic probe materials, such as being easy to recycle and addressing the solubility issues faced by organic probes. However, in many instances, the immobilization of the organic fluorophore on the substrate surface is complex, and using expensive organic fluorophores is another issue with such materials. Therefore, quantum dots as an alternative fluorescence tag have attracted attention due to several advantages [11,12]. The main advantage of quantum dots is that they can be quickly immobilized on the material’s surface compared to the covalent functionalization of organic fluorophores. Such composite materials require a mesoporous matrix where the quantum dots can be accommodated for dual functions of sensing and adsorptions. Silica as a robust matrix is well studied for its tailored porous structure, stability, high surface area, tunable pore size, and biocompatibility. Similarly, carbon dots (CDs) as the fluorescent tag have attracted attention due to their tunable photoluminescence, brighter emission, photostability, water solubility, simple preparation, and low toxicity. Given these unique features, CDs have been widely used for metal ion detection, photocatalysis, and biosensing applications [13–19]. CDs often undergo aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in aqueous media, limiting their sensory and adsorption applications. Therefore, various methods have been established to protect CDs from aggregation-induced quenching and adsorptions, such as coating and immobilization in protective matrices [20–22]. Among different ways, CDs immobilized in mesoporous silica matrices have demonstrated multiple advantages, such as addressing aggregation, excitation-independent emission, and enhanced adsorption properties [23]. In general, most CDs exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence, which restricts their application in sensory applications. Conversely, CD-immobilized silica or composites (SCD) have shown limited excitation dependency photoluminescence due to the tuned growth of CDs in the pores of the silica matrix. Therefore, engineered CDs/mSiO2 nanocomposites have been fabricated using various techniques, such as grafting or doping in a silica matrix, to construct a single nanostructure for multiple applications. Recently, Chen et al. have shown that the blue emissive carbon dots encapsulated in SBA-15 material showed good adsorption and detection behavior towards uranium ions [24]. Zhang et al. have applied mesoporous silica-assembled carbon dots for the adsorption of 2,4-dichlorophenol and metal ions [25]. Yang et al. synthesized the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD)-embedded SiO2 to study the tunable luminescence properties [26]. Recently, Zhang et al. have applied amino-functionalized mesoporous silica anchored carbon dots for Cr(VI) detection in environmental water [27]. Xia et al. have immobilized carbon quantum dots in MCM-41 to detect Hg2+ ions [28]. Recently, Hu et al. have demonstrated delayed fluorescence with F, N-doped carbon dots confined in silica [29]. A close analysis of the literature on such materials shows that most materials were designed for either detection or removal functions separately. Also, most of the probes are designed to deliver a fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, most CDs exhibit blue luminescence under UV excitations, which is harmful to living organisms, limiting their bio-applications. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence is another demerit. The application of CDs/mSiO2 for simultaneous metal ion detection and removal is scarce.Table 1. .

Given these facts, we aimed to develop red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) doped dendritic fibrous nano-silica (DFNS) material for heavy metal ion detection and adsorption. DFNS was chosen due to its 3D morphology, fibrous structure, high surface area, and better aqueous dispersion for sensitive detection [30–35]. Also, the presence of radially accessible pores can facilitate the easy immobilization of CDs in the nano-channels of the DFNS. The red emissive carbon dots are chosen due to the low damaging effect of the excitation conditions, which has merits in bio-sensing applications [14,15,36]. The covalent immobilization of alkyl thiol on fibrous silica was for targeting mercury ions through a turn-on fluorescence. The strong binding affinity of thiols towards mercury is well-known and examined for mercury remediation [37]. Most CD-based materials usually show fluorescence quenching in the presence of metal ions. However, it is reported that the disruption to radiative recombinations in quantum dots leads to fluorescence quenching by both the electron acceptor and electron donor [38,39]. Therefore, we wanted to use the above phenomena to construct a turn-off–on probe material. Our hypothesis was the incorporation of CDs into the alkyl thiol functionalized fibrous silica probably will initially show a weak fluorescence due to the electron transfer from thiols to CDs. Subsequently, in the presence of mercury ions, the thiol-mercury strong binding interaction will hinder the above electron transfer process and lead to the recovery of the fluorescence of CDs.

Thus, in this work, we successfully synthesized a high surface area dendritic fibrous nano-silica (DFNS) material using a modified technique. Subsequently, we covalently immobilized alkyl thiols onto the DFNS, resulting in the formation of DFNS@SH. We introduced red-emissive carbon dots into the material through hydrothermal processes to further enhance its sensing capabilities, creating the composite material CD@DFNS@SH. As per the objectives, this material exhibits specific and sensitive turn-on red emission upon exposure to mercury ions. In contrast to carbon dots, which typically show turn-off behavior, our composite material displays turn-on behavior. The red-emission of the material is biocompatible and has been effectively utilized as a biosensor in Artemia salina, serving as animal models. The material's excitation independency offers an advantage as it enables using a single excitation wavelength. Additionally, the material possesses a high adsorption capacity and can be recycled easily, making it suitable for sustainable remediation processes.

The synthesis of fibrous silica, its covalent functionalization with alkyl thiols, and the post-synthesis doping with red-emissive carbon dots are depicted in Scheme 1 and detailed in the experimental section. The red emissive carbon dots were synthesized in the ionic liquid medium (1-Butyl-3-Methylimidazolium Chloride) following a literature method [40]. After synthesizing carbon dots, the high surface area solid matrix as fibrous silica was synthesized in a biphasic emulsion system following our previous report with some modifications [30]. The change of solvent system (nitrobenzene / H2O) and urea as a hydrolyzing agent, keeping the other parameters intact, gives DFNS a smaller particle size of ∼ 50 nm with a high surface area of 1080 m2/g as compared to the previous report. The fibrous silica was then covalently attached to the alkyl thiols using (3-mercaptopropyl)trimethoxysilane to get the DFNS@SH material. The above material is then doped with red carbon dots by refluxing the mixtures in ethanol to get the final material CD@DFNS@SH.

The surface and structural analysis of the synthesized materials was done using Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) & Brunauer Emmet & Teller (BET) measurements. The TEM images of the CDs synthesized in ionic liquid as a processing medium yielded discrete, uniform-sized carbon dots with an average particle size of 5–7 nm obtained by the differential light scattering method (DLS) (Fig. S1A). The interplanar distance was found to be 0.22 nm, consistent with graphene (100) faced spacing (Fig. 1 A–C). The distinctly visible fringes show the high crystallinity of the carbon dots evident from the HR-TEM images (Fig. 1C). The other relevant characterization data of CDs are provided in Supporting Information (Fig. S2).

The SEM images of the synthesized fibrous silica (DFNS) indicate the regular and spherical morphology. The morphology remains unchanged during the chemical processing from DFNS to DFNS@SH and CD@DFNS@SH (Fig. 2 A–C). The TEM & HR-TEM images (Fig. 2D–F) also revealed the regular fibrous morphology having a particle size of ∼ 50 nm supported by the DLS measurement profile (Fig. S1B). The slight increase in the particle size of CD@DFNS@SH materials may be due to surface functionalization and some aggregation. The doping of CDs in the radial channels of the DFNS@SH leads to a slight change in the contrast and looks denser in TEM images of CD@DFNS@SH. The BET analysis (N2 sorption) shows a surface area of ∼ 1080 m2/g for DFNS with sharp capillary condensation, typical for type IV isotherms (Fig. 3 A). The surface area subsequently reduced during alkyl thiol functionalization (∼425 m2/g) in DFNS@SH (type IV isotherms). The surface area was further reduced with doping of CDs (10 m2/g) to form CD@DFNS@SH, which shows no capillary condensation (type II isotherm). The reduction in pore size & volume also confirms the covalent functionalization of alkyl thiols and loading of CDs in the final composite material (Fig. 3B). Probably, the radial accessibility of the pore channels in fibrous silica allows effective immobilization of CDs.

The broad angle PXRD profiles of the DFNS and the final material show amorphous nature, having bands at 22.67° & 24.71°, remaining similar throughout the chemical processing (Fig. 3C). The TGA analysis of the materials was thoroughly investigated to know the stability of composite material (Fig. 3D). The phase up to ∼ 250 °C suggests the loss of any form of physically adsorbed water. The thermal profile of DFNS shows nearly no loss and high thermal stability of the material. The thermal profile in the region 250–550 °C corresponds to the loss of organics with a weight loss of 13.21 % for DFNS@SH material and 51.2 % for the final material CD@DFNS@SH. The increased weight loss compared to the starting material (DFNS) confirms the functionalization of thiol functionality and the loading of CDs to form CD@DFNS@SH material. The 550 to 800 °C thermal profile corresponds to the dihydroxylation of surface silanol groups.

The surface and chemical analysis of the materials were also elucidated using XPS spectroscopy (Fig. 5 & Fig. S3–S5). The synthesis of fibrous silica was also supported by XPS spectra which can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 534.1 & 532.84 eV, corresponding to the Si-OH & Si-O-Si linkage (Fig. S3). In the XPS analysis of carbon dots (Fig. S4A–C), the C 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into four peaks assigned to sp2 carbons C = C (284.5 eV), sp3 carbons (C-N/C-O, 286.5 eV), carboxy carbons (288.5 eV) & carbonyl carbons (287.5 eV) (Fig. S4A). The N 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks resembling pyridinic N (398.3 eV), amino N (399.1) & pyrrolic N (400.2 eV). Also, the O 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.5 eV (carbonyl, C = O) & 533.1 eV for C-O/C-OH, respectively (Fig. S4B, C). Conclusively, the surface states of the red emissive carbon dots are rich in COOH, and NH2 groups have pyridinic N content with the graphitic core. Similarly, the XPS analysis of the materials DFNS@SH and CD@DFNS@SH gave important information about the functionalization of SH functionality & loading of carbon dots to form the final material (Fig. S5 & Fig. 5). The C 1 s XPS spectra of the material DFNS@SH can be deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to C-Si linkage (283.5 eV), C-C (285.4 eV) & C-S linkage (286.5 eV) which confirms the covalent attachment of SH group. Also, the presence of silica can be verified by the Si 2p XPS spectra having peaked at 103.8 eV (Fig. S5A, B). The C 1 s spectra of the final material CD@DFNS@SH can be deconvoluted into five peaks representing C-Si linkage (283.5 eV), C = C/C-C (284.5 eV), C-N/C-S (286.5 eV), COOH groups (288.5 eV) and carbonyl groups (287.5 eV) (Fig. 5A). The N 1 s XPS spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks 398.3, 399.1 & 400.2 eV resembling pyridine N, amino N, and pyrrole N, also the O 1 s can be deconvoluted into two peaks referring to the C = O & C-O / C-OH at 533.1 &531.5 functionalities respectively. The intactness of the silica can be confirmed by the XPS spectra of Si 2p having a peak at 103.4 eV (Fig. 5B, C). To our observation from the above chemical analysis, the red emissive carbon dots are rich in amino and COOH groups having pyridinic N content with the graphitic core.

The optical selectivity studies of the material CD@DFNS@SH were carried out at a neutral aqueous buffer medium at pH ∼ 7.4 using UV–Vis and fluorescence studies. The UV–Vis spectrum of the aqueous suspension of the material (5 mg / 20 mL) shows two prominent bands at 499 nm and 325 nm. In the presence of various metal ions, the UV–Vis spectra show changes with only mercury ions, indicating selectivity (Fig. 6 A).

Under fluorescence studies, we initially assessed the material's excitation dependency by conducting experiments with varying excitation wavelengths centered around 500 nm. It is observed that a constant emission band centered at 645 nm appears irrespective of the excitation wavelength indicating excitation independency (Fig. S6B), and all the fluorescence studies were carried out with an excitation wavelength of 500 nm. The fluorescence stability of the CDs and material (CD@DFNS@SH) was also monitored with time. It displays that the fluorescence emission of CDs decreases with time, indicating aggregation behavior (Fig. S7). On the other hand, the composite material shows a stable fluorescence intensity over time, revealing the non-aggregation behavior of CDs under the silica matrix (Fig. S8). The fluorescence studies of the composite material in the presence of various metal ions show a turn-on behavior with mercury ions. In contrast, no significant changes were perceived with other metal ions indicating specificity (Fig. 6B, C).

The various sensing parameters were derived from the fluorescence titration experiments (Fig. 6D) with the increasing concentration of the Hg2+ ions. The gradual addition of Hg2+ up to saturation concentration was considered for the calculation of the fluorescence enhancement constant (Ks) following a non-linear equation (Fig. 6E) [44]. For the LOD (limit of detection) calculations, low concentrations of Hg2+ were added with incremental addition and were calculated using the 3σ method (Fig. 6F), where 3σ = 3 × S.D / Slope, S.D is the standard deviation of the probe material obtained from the three blank fluorescence reading (S.D = 5) & S is the slope obtained from the LOD plot of Intensity vs. Hg2+ concentrations [6]. The interference studies within the linear range with mercury ions and an excess of other metal ions show similar fluorescence behavior to control experiments with only mercury-ion (Fig. 6G). The addition of mercury-ion exhibits colour intensification, both under UV and naked eye observations (Fig. 6E inset & Fig. S9). The stability of the material in the aqueous phase was also monitored for one week to show the stability of the material (Fig. 6H). The lifetime measurements of the material alone (3.6 ns) and in the presence of mercury-ion (4.2 ns) validate the turn-on fluorescence behavior (Fig. 6I). The fluorescence enhancement constant (Ks), LOD (limit of detection), LOQ (limit of quantification) & the linear range values were calculated to be as 4.25 × 105 M−1, 5 nM, 16.8 µM & 0.016 – 2 nM which were compared with other materials (Table 2 ).

The material's affinity for mercury ions can be attributed to thiol functionality in the DFNS, which can be rationalized by the HSAB (hard and soft acids and bases) concept. Thiols, also known as mercaptans (meaning 'capturing mercury'), are considered soft coordinating groups that prefer coordination with soft metals like mercury. The literature has reported that thiols have a higher binding affinity for mercury ions than other metal ions such as Cd2+, Pb2+, Pd2+, and others [45]. Based on our strategy, a turn-on fluorescence with mercury was observed, which is unusual with CDs only [17,18]. Initially, due to the presence of the electron donor thiol groups, the radiative recombination in CDs is disturbed due to the electron transfer from thiol to CDs. As a result, a weak fluorescence is observed. In the presence of mercury ions, the thiol groups bind to the mercury ions, and the PET (photoinduced electron transfer) process breaks down, leading to the recovery of the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 7 ), showing a turn-on fluorescence. The phenomenon of metal chelation-enhanced fluorescence is widely documented in the literature. To further support this mechanism, we monitored the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) in the presence of thiol-functionalized DFNS material (DFNS@SH), which exhibited fluorescence quenching (Fig. S10A). However, the extent of quenching is less pronounced compared to the CD@DFNS@SH composite material, where the CDs within the nano-channels are in close proximity, enabling efficient photoinduced electron transfer operations (Fig. S10A). Notably, we observed no overlap between the absorption band of DFNS@SH and the emission band of CDs, thus excluding the possibility of fluorescence energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. S10B). Additionally, there were no observable shifts in bands, dismissing the internal charge transfer (ICT) mechanism. Consequently, PET was identified as the operative process responsible for the fluorescence weakening or quenching in the composite material (CD@DFNS@SH). We also investigated the fluorescence behavior of CDs in the presence of mercury ions, which also exhibited fluorescence quenching. This quenching can be attributed to the binding interaction between the surface functional groups of CDs (such as amines and carboxylates) and the mercury ions. Therefore, the fluorescence turn-on behavior of the composite material (CD@DFNS@SH) in the presence of mercury ions can be explained as the breakdown of chelation-induced PET, leading to the recovery of fluorescence in the CDs, which is also supported by the TCSPC (time-correlated single photon counting) experiments (Fig. 6I).

In addition to the sensitive detection of mercury ions, mercury remediation is also vital for environmental benefits. Therefore, the batch adsorption studies [6] were also performed by taking 5 mg of the material (CD@DFNS@SH) in varying mercury-ion concentrations (20 to 200 ppm) in an aqueous buffer medium. The suspensions were shaken for 4 h, centrifuged, and syringe filtered (0.45 µm), and the filtrate was analyzed by ICP-MS analysis. The equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) was calculated using the equation Qe = (Ci - Cf) V / W, where Ci and Cf are the initial and final concentration of Hg2+ ions, Qe is the equilibrium adsorption capacity in mg g−1, V is the volume in litre (L), and W represents the weight of the adsorbent in gram (g). The fitting of the adsorption isotherm plot gives the maximum adsorption capacity (Qe) of 695 mg g−1 for mercury-ions (Fig. 8 A). The high adsorption capacity may be due to the combined effect of thiol groups and the surface functional groups of carbon dots in the composite material. Also, to predict the adsorption behavior of the material for Hg2+ ions, Langmuir and Freundlich’s plots were derived from the experimental data, which shows that the Hg2+ observes a monolayer adsorption behavior having a good correlation factor close to unity well-agreed with Langmuir model (Fig. 8B & Fig. S11). The selective adsorption behavior of the material was also examined in the presence of coexisting ions. For this, an equivalent amount of the material (5 mg) was mixed with the 40-ppm mixed metal ion solution. After adsorption, the filtrate was analyzed with ICP-MS analysis, and the material was washed with water and analyzed with EDX (Energy dispersive X-ray) analysis. The removal percentage (% R) from the ICP-MS analysis was calculated using the formula % R = Ci - Cf / Ci × 100 and tabulated in Table S3 [6]. The EDX spectrum of material after adsorption shows the signals of Si, O, C, N, S, Hg, and Cl elements showing selective Hg2+ ion adsorption (Fig. S12). A pH-dependent study (pH; 2–10) was also carried out to check the influence of pH on the adsorption of Hg2+ ions (Fig. S13). It was observed that the adsorption of Hg2+ was maximum in the pH range of 5 to 8 and minimum at lower & higher pH values (2–4 & >8). The minimum adsorption at lower pH values is possibly due to the protonation of surface functionalities of CDs and thiols in the final material (CD@DFNS@SH). At higher pH values (>8), the formation of hydroxides & oxides predominates. At neutral to basic pH, the thiolate and the carbon dots’ deprotonated surface functionalities strongly bind the Hg2+ ions responsible for higher adsorption.

The adsorption kinetics was also explored to realize the adsorption rate of Hg2+ ions from a concentration of 30 ppm until the equilibrium was reached (Fig. 9 ). It is observed that around 80 % adsorption occurs within 5 min and ≥ 98 % occurs within 20 min (Fig. 9A, C). The rate of adsorption was evaluated using both pseudo-first-order

t
q e
as depicted in Fig. 9B (inset) and Fig. S14. In these equations, qe (mg g−1) is the quantity of Hg2+ adsorbed per unit mass of the adsorbent at equilibrium, and qt (mg g−1) is the adsorbed Hg2+ at time t, whereas k1 (min−1) and k2 (g mg−1 min−1) are the rate constants of the pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order interactions [46]. The kinetic parameters data are summarized in Table 3 . The sorption capacities calculated from the pseudo-second-order kinetics are close to the experimental value. The correlation factor is closer to unity suggesting the adsorption kinetics follow a second-order interaction mechanism, hinting the adsorption process is necessarily a chemisorption.

The thermodynamic aspects of the adsorption of Hg2+ ions by the material were studied at six different temperatures 293, 298, 303, 308, 313 & 318 K, keeping other factors constant. It was observed that the temperature has a positive effect on the adsorption of the Hg2+ ions with the increase in equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe) from 50 mg g−1 at 293 K to 315 mg g−1 at 313 K. This marks the adsorption of Hg2+ ions by the material is endothermic & having a positive role of temperature. Furthermore, the Van’t Hoff equation (log (qe / Ce) = ΔS / 2.303R – ΔH / 2.303RT) & Gibbs free energy (ΔG = ΔH – TΔS) was analyzed to determine the thermodynamic parameters. In the equations, ΔS is the entropy change (J mol−1 K−1), ΔH is the enthalpy change (J mol−1 K−1), R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), qe / Ce is the adsorption affinity & ΔG is the Gibbs free energy. The ΔH & ΔS were determined from the slope and intercept of the plot of log (qe / Ce) vs. 1/T and found to be 224.10 & 0.77 kJ mol−1. Also, the ΔG values at different temperatures were determined (Fig. S15A & Table 4
). The positive & high heat of enthalpy and entropy suggests the strong interaction of the material with the Hg2+ ions confirming the chemisorption adsorption process, which is endothermic. Also, the negative ΔG values suggest the adsorption process is spontaneous and favorable. The effect of ionic strength (NaCl) was also investigated on the adsorption of Hg2+ ions by the material at the neutral pH, it was observed that no significant change was obtained at low ionic strength, but the adsorption efficiency was slightly decreased at higher ionic strength (∼0.8 to 1.2 M NaCl), this interference possibly due to Cl− ions which has affected the adsorption efficiency by complexing with the Hg (II) (Fig. S15B).

Recyclability is an important aspect of functional material that has significance in designing sustainable systems. Therefore, apart from the detection and adsorption studies, the recyclability studies were also performed using UV–Vis and fluorescence studies (Fig. 10 & Fig. S16). Initially, the UV–Vis spectra of the material (Fig. 10A-a) and the mercury ions adsorbed material were recorded after washings with water. The UV–Vis spectra of Hg2+ ions adsorbed material showed a decrease in absorbance of 499 nm band and blue shifting of 325 nm band (Fig. 10A-b). Subsequently, the mercury ion adsorbed material was treated with 0.5 N HCl solution for elution [47] and subjected to UV–Vis spectral recording showing a regaining intensity of 499 nm absorbance band due to the striping of mercury ion (Fig. 10A-c). The changes in the 325 nm band may be due to the protonation of amine functional groups of CDs. After base treatment (0.5 N NaOH) and washings, the UV–Vis spectra showed the regeneration of the original bands (Fig. 10A-d). This confirms the easy regeneration of the material towards mercury ions via acid-base treatments. Furthermore, fluorescence spectroscopy also tracked the reversibility, using the acid-base treatments and with EDTA for the material's recyclability (Fig. S16A, B). The recyclability was performed for five cycles, and adsorption capacity was also monitored to show similar adsorption capacities (Fig. 10B). The regenerated material was characterized thoroughly, and to our observation, the material maintains its structural integrity and functionality similar to that of the original material (Fig. S17A, B).

Fluorescence imaging was taken to check the potential utility of the probe to track mercury ions in real living organisms like brine shrimp Artemia salina (detailed in the experimental section) [7,48]. Typically, the brine shrimp Artemia nauplii were treated with 50 µL of the probe material for 2 h, and the bright field images were taken to show the accumulation of the probe material in the GI (Gastro Intestinal) tract of the Artemia (Fig. 11 A, B). The fluorescence images displayed the red staining of the GI tract of the Artemia under a UV filter due to the presence of the probe material (Fig. 11C–E). Then the Artemia was treated with 20, 30 & 40 µM of the 10−6 M Hg2+ ion solution for 40 min. Again the fluorescence images were taken, which shows the increased intensity of the red staining, similar to the turn-on behavior of the probe in the presence of mercury ions (Fig. 11F–H). Conclusively this validated the biosensing performance of the probe material towards Hg2+ ions in animal models, which can be helpful for monitoring applications.

The critical feature of sustainable systems is their applicability in real samples; therefore, to demonstrate the practical relevance of the material in real examples, we have utilized Industrial wastewater as a real sample. Initially, 5 mg of the material was added to 50 mL of the Milli-Q water and industrial sewage separately, followed by sonication for 20 min to achieve dispersion. After sonication, the fluorescence emission spectra of the solutions were recorded. The sewage solution was further spiked with a known concentration of the Hg2+ ion solutions (Fig. S18A), and spectra were recorded. The concentrations were estimated from the calibration plots of fluorescence titration (Fig. S18B). The results show recoveries around 101 to 105 %, with a relative standard deviation below 5 %, showing the potential use of the material as a probe for detecting Hg2+ in actual sample systems (Table S4).

Typically, 5 g (28.63 mmol) of [Bmim]Cl was heated at 90 °C for 15 min, to which 1 g (8.61 mmol) of Levulinic acid, 1.2 g (10.15 mmol) of p-Phenylene diamine, and 1.2 g (19.98 mmol) of urea were added and further heated for 4 h at 90 °C. The viscous liquid that appeared red in color was cooled down to room temperature and dialyzed (2 k Da MWCO) against water two times for 10 h and collected by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 5 min. The red-colored crude compound was monitored on silica plates (TLC) and showed some impurity fractions along with the intense red-colored products. The red-colored crude was further purified using column chromatography and freeze-dried to get a dark red-colored fluffy compound. FTIR, Raman, PXRD, TGA & TEM analysis characterized the red carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sub nanometer dendritic fibrous nano silica (DFNS)
ce:section.ce:para: The 50 nm dendritic fibrous nano-silica was synthesized using a biphasic emulsion system, typically 1 g (2.60 mmol) of cetyl pyridinium bromide (CPB) was added to 200 mL Milli-Q water and stirred at 500 rpm for 15 min. After that, 2 g (26.27 mmol) of thiourea dissolved in 50 mL water was added to the above solution, and the reaction mixture was stirred for another 30 min, followed by adding 3 mL of triethylamine. To the above solution mixture, 5 mL (22.56 mmol) TEOS in 30 mL Nitrobenzene was added slowly for 45 min under constant stirring at 40 °C temperature. After the addition, the cloudy white suspension was observed, and the reaction mixture was heated at 80 °C for 5 h. The white precipitates obtained were filtered and washed with water and methanol several times. The template was removed by refluxing it in the 150 mL HCl / Water mixture three times at 85 °C for 4 h, and finally filtered, centrifuged, and vacuum dried, obtaining 4.26 g of the pure white-colored compound as dendritic fibrous nano-silica (DFNS).

For the covalent attachment of (3-mercaptopropyl)trimethoxysilane (MPTMS), 2 g of the above compound (DFNS) was refluxed with 3 mL (16.04 mmol) of MPTES in 150 mL dry toluene at 85 °C for 12 h under argon. The reaction mixture was cooled down and filtered to collect the product. The product was washed three times with methanol and chloroform and finally dried in an oven for five h at 60 °C, affording 2.12 g of the compound designated as DFNS@SH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of carbon dots and synthesis of the final material CD@DFNS@SH
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 2 g of the material DFNS@SH was dispersed in 150 mL Milli-Q water, followed by the addition of red carbon dots solution (50 mg / 50 mL ethanol). The solution mixture was refluxed at 78 °C for 6 h under argon, and the red-colored compound was collected by filtration, washed thoroughly with water, ethanol, and chloroform, and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 3 h designated as CD@DFNS@SH material which was thoroughly characterized by various techniques.

The photophysical studies were conducted in a dispersion medium (5 mg / 50 mL water) and mixed well with the analytes using a vortex shaker. The shaking was repeated in each experiment, and the spectra were recorded. The analytes used were chloride & oxide salts of cations (10−3 M) viz Li+, Na+, K+, Cs+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+ & Hg2+ the UV–Vis spectra were recorded between 200 and 800 nm range. The fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 500 nm and emission at 510 nm, respectively. The 3σ method was used for the LOD calculations with small incremental addition of the specific cation in the titration experiment.

For adsorption studies, batch adsorption experiments were performed by taking 5 mg of the final material in 50 mL of various metal ion concentrations (20–200 ppm). The test tubes were shaken for 3 h to reach the maximum adsorption equilibrium; after the treatment times, the sample tubes were centrifuged and syringe filtered using a micron filter (0.45 µm). The concentrations were analyzed using ICP-MS. The equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe), Langmuir, Freundlich plots & kinetics parameters were determined. The equilibrium adsorption capacity was determined using the following equation Qe = (Ci – Cf) V / W, where Qe (mg g−1) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity, Ci & Cf are the initial and final concentrations (mg L−1), V is the volume of the analyte (L), and W is the weight of the adsorbent (g). For removal studies, 50 mL of 40 ppm mixed analytes (competitive adsorption) & 30 ppm of specific analyte (adsorption rate kinetics) were treated with an equal amount of adsorbent (5 mg) and shaken for 3 h to reach the equilibrium. Afterward, the tubes were centrifuged, syringe filtered, and finally submitted to ICP-MS to know the final concentrations of the Hg2+ ions. The equation used is % R = Ci - Cf / Ci × 100, where % R is the removal percentage, Ci & Cf are the initial and final concentrations of the Hg2+ ions. For adsorption rate kinetics, pseudo-first and second-order kinetics were analyzed.

For biosensing applications, Artemia nauplii was considered the cost-effective animal model for experimental studies. The A. salina cysts were hatched overnight under aerobic conditions, and larvae (Artemia nauplii) produced from the cysts were used further for in vivo imaging studies. Approximately 40 Artemia nauplii were taken in 20 mL of brine solution, incubated with the 50 µL probe material (20 mg/25 mL), and left for 3 h. After the incubation, the glass sides were prepared with these organisms and viewed under the BX53 OLYMPUS microscope using bright & fluorescent filters. Afterward, the 20, 30 & 40 µM of (10−6 M) Hg2+ ion solution were added, and again the images were taken using the microscope.

In summary, this work demonstrates the preparation of carbon dot doped fibrous silica material as a single platform for dual mercury ion detection and remediation functions. The composite material with alkyl thiol functionality for mercury-ion specificity and red-emissive carbon dots as fluorescence tags displayed outstanding optical properties and enabled turn-on probing of mercury-ion. The thiol functionality on mesoporous silica efficiently weakens the fluorescence of CDs by a PET mechanism. In the presence of mercury-ion, due to the strong binding affinity of thiols towards the mercury-ion, the PET breaks down, leading to the recovery of fluorescence of CDs, showing a turn-on behavior with nanomolar sensitivity in the red region. This is in contrast to CD-based materials, which often show fluorescence quenching. The excitation independency of the red-emissive CDs omits the blue emission and has advantages in biosensing applications in Artemia salina through fluorescence imaging. This material as a fluorescence probe used to quantify mercury in a real sample very precisely. This material also shows a very high adsorption capacity for mercury ions following pseudo-second-order kinetics and can be recycled easily. The simultaneous application of the material towards environmental monitoring and remediation with biosensing application is advantageous.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S.Y, N.C, V.S, A. R. P acknowledge the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Govt. of India, for the financial support (MLP 0055). S.Y. and N.C acknowledge CSIR & AcSIR for fellowship & Ph.D. degree. ADCIF division of CSIR-CSMCRI is acknowledged for providing analytical facilities. A CSIR-CSMCRI communication no 57/2023.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144715.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mercury binding sites in thiol-functionalized mesostructured silica
